text: The surgeon uses robotic instruments to work on the preperitoneal flap during the repair of an incarcerated right inguinal hernia. The tissue appears adherent around the infraepigastric vessels, requiring careful manipulation to create space and reorient the surgical approach.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue in the supravesicular space, pulling the pocket away from the abdominal wall to avoid drifting into it, with minimal lateral fat visible.

The surgeon adjusts the camera to a right lateral oblique angle to enhance visualization. Using electrocautery, the surgeon buzzes directly on the hernia sac, carefully pulling surrounding structures and fat to allow them to separate and fall away.

The surgeon uses electrocautery on the hernia sac, pushing fat laterally to expose the edge of the sac. The vas deferens becomes visible, accompanied by the removal of a small cord lipoma. The vas deferens is seen on the right side of the surgical field.
The surgeon opens up the fat to orient towards the Cooper's ligament and pubic tubercles, clearly visualizing the edge of the hernia sac while searching for a cord lipoma.

The surgeon carefully navigates around the corner, ensuring to stay clear of the iliac region during bilateral suprapubic fixation. The view shows high medial muscles with no identifiable vascular structures obstructing the surgical field.
